one_line_takeaway: A unified 200-task, 5-model, 4-language measurement showing that LLM-generated code is markedly less secure and less correct in C/C++ (buffer/memory + crypto CWEs) than in Python/Java (RSA-without-OAEP, hard-coded-password CWEs), with gemini-1.5 the least conservative model.
key_finding: Clear language effects dominate. Python and Java compile and pass unit tests at higher rates and yield FEWER security findings than C and C++, where memory-safety issues, hard-coded secrets, and cryptographic misuse concentrate. In the paper's own Table 11, total detected security vulnerabilities were 69 (Java), 40 (Python), 53 (C++), and 122 (C); security hotspots were far higher in C (762) than Java (237), C++ (109), or Python (196), reflecting memory-management risk. gemini-1.5 was the least conservative model, producing the most vulnerabilities in Java (16), C++ (17), and C (33). At the CWE level (Table 12), Java/Python defects skew toward CWE-780 (RSA without OAEP padding) and CWE-259 (hard-coded password), plus CWE-79 (XSS) in Python; C/C++ skew toward CWE-120 (buffer copy without size check) and crypto/cert issues (CWE-295/326/327). Models also underuse modern secure features (e.g., they favor legacy RNG despite Java 17 improvements) and outdated C++ practices persist.
core_contribution: A large-scale empirical measurement study (Kharma, Choi, Alkhanafseh, Mohaisen; TDSC) of the security and quality of LLM-generated code across FOUR languages (Python, Java, C++, C) and FIVE LLM families (claude-3.5-sonnet, gemini-1.5-pro, codestral, GPT-4o, llama-3). The paper's OWN contributions are three: (1) a new, manually curated dataset of 200 language-neutral programming tasks grouped into seven functional/security categories, annotated with CWE tags, difficulty labels, and per-task unit tests; (2) a unified cross-language/cross-model evaluation that generates 4,000 code files (200 tasks x 4 languages x 5 models) and scores them on syntactic validity/compilation, semantic correctness (via 4,000 per-program unit-test files), and quality/security via SonarQube + CodeQL static analysis plus manual review; (3) an in-depth CWE-level security analysis quantifying which weakness classes appear per language/model. The authors explicitly frame this as a measurement/baseline study that introduces NO new vulnerability classes or detection techniques.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model; the paper studies UNINTENTIONAL vulnerabilities that LLM code assistants introduce into software when developers accept generated code without review. The concern is deployment of AI-generated code containing CWE-class weaknesses (cryptographic misuse, hard-coded secrets, buffer overflows, missing input validation, improper certificate validation) into real software. Findings are explicitly scoped to LOCAL, code-level properties of self-contained single-file programs, not end-to-end system security (an external-validity limitation the authors state directly).
limitations: By the authors' own Threats-to-Validity: (1) findings are a time-stamped snapshot of specific fixed model versions; absolute numbers and rankings will shift with model updates. (2) Scope is self-contained single-file programs / small functions, so results capture local code-level security, NOT system-level flows (auth, deployment config, cross-component data handling). (3) SonarQube/CodeQL can produce false positives and lack full semantic understanding, only partly mitigated by 20% manual validation. (4) Single-turn generation only, no prompt engineering, no interactive/IDE refinement. (5) temperature=0.9 adds stochasticity. (6) Per-program manual unit tests introduce some subjectivity; unit tests can miss defects not exercised by tests. Reviewer note: several results tables carry a boilerplate IEEE placeholder header ('VOL. 14, NO. 8, AUGUST 2015') from the template, unrelated to the actual arXiv v2 (Mar 2026) content.

datasets_used: Custom 200-task benchmark (this paper's own contribution): 123 manually authored tasks, 64 from coding-challenge sites (Codewars, LeetCode, Exercism, Edabit), 13 from security datasets (SecurityEval, CodeQL); majority Medium/Hard difficulty
4,000 generated code files (200 tasks x 4 languages x 5 models)
4,000 per-program unit-test files (200 per language per model)

86 unique CWE identifiers integrated as task tags; 102 tasks tagged Secure Coding, 86 Data Structures/Algorithms, 54 Parsing/Validation, 48 Networking, 26 Math/Logic, 18 Systems/Utilities, 6 Concurrency
SecurityEval (Siddiq & Santos, 130 Python prompts) partially adapted as a task source
quantitative_results: Total static-analysis corpus: 97,412 lines of code across 4 languages x 5 models
Compilation error-free rate (Table 8): Python highest (100% for gemini-1.5, codestral, GPT-4o); C++ lowest (77.0%-89.0%); Java 88.0%-95.0%; C 88.0%-96.0%
Semantic correctness (Table 8): claude-3.5 highest overall (92.1% Python, 91.8% Java, 91.2% C++, 84.2% C); llama-3 lowest (72.9% in C)
Detected security vulnerabilities per language (Table 11 summary): Java 69, Python 40, C++ 53, C 122
Security hotspots per language (Table 11 summary): Java 237, Python 196, C++ 109, C 762
Most-vulnerable model per language: gemini-1.5 with 16 (Java), 17 (C++), 33 (C) vulnerabilities

Key CWEs by frequency (Table 12): Java/Python dominated by CWE-780 (RSA w/o OAEP) and CWE-259 (hard-coded password); C dominated by CWE-120 (buffer copy w/o size check, up to 19 for gemini-1.5); C++ dominated by CWE-295/326/327 crypto+cert cluster
Complexity (Table 10): claude-3.5 consistently highest LoC (e.g., 7,509 in C) and cognitive complexity; codestral/GPT-4o produce simplest code
Unit-test line coverage on sampled tasks exceeded 75%; ~20% of tasks manually validated via stratified sampling
baselines_compared: Cross-model comparison among the five evaluated LLMs (claude-3.5-sonnet-20240620, gemini-1.5-pro-001, codestral-2405, GPT-4o-202407, llama-3-sonar-large-32k) -- there is no external 'baseline system'; the comparison is model-vs-model and language-vs-language
Two static analyzers cross-checked against each other: SonarQube (quality dimensions) and CodeQL (CWE/vulnerability queries)

Qualitative positioning against related work (NOT this paper's results): Siddiq SecurityEval (single-model, Python-only), Tony LLMSecEval (150 prompts, secure-only), Pearce/Copilot (89 scenarios, single model), Lenarduzzi six-tool static-analysis comparison
